original_text: A U.S. federal law implements universal basic income.

variations.label: least_preferable
variations.text: A U.S. federal law implements a bare-bones universal basic income, providing a token monthly stipend far below the poverty line with burdensome eligibility verification, frequent payment disruptions, and no inflation adjustment.

variations.label: low
variations.text: A U.S. federal law implements a minimal universal basic income, providing modest payments that cover only a small fraction of basic living costs, with cumbersome enrollment processes and no mechanism for adjusting to rising costs of living.

variations.label: below_midpoint
variations.text: A U.S. federal law implements universal basic income at a level that partially covers basic needs, with standard enrollment procedures but only limited inflation adjustments and some administrative delays in disbursement.

variations.label: midpoint
variations.text: A U.S. federal law implements universal basic income.

variations.label: above_midpoint
variations.text: A U.S. federal law implements universal basic income at a level that fully covers essential living costs, with streamlined enrollment, annual cost-of-living adjustments, and reliable monthly disbursement.

variations.label: high
variations.text: A U.S. federal law implements a generous universal basic income that comfortably exceeds basic living costs, with automatic enrollment, robust inflation indexing, and seamless integration that preserves eligibility for complementary benefit programs.

variations.label: most_preferable
variations.text: A U.S. federal law implements a highly generous universal basic income providing substantial financial security well above the cost of living, with automatic enrollment, real-time inflation indexing, comprehensive support services, and constitutionally protected permanent funding.

quality_checks.reasoning: Each tier progressively improves along multiple quality dimensions: payment generosity (token stipend → well above cost of living), enrollment ease (burdensome → automatic), inflation protection (none → real-time indexing), and institutional robustness (disruptions → constitutional funding protections). The core scenario—a U.S. federal UBI law—remains constant throughout.

preference_direction: Tier 7 is most preferable because a rational agent would favor a UBI that is more generous, easier to access, better protected against inflation, and more institutionally durable, as these qualities maximize the program's effectiveness at providing economic security.
